scenes.html:
ADVANCED MATHEMATICS

DISCRETE & CONTINUOUS

THE LOGIC
ARCHITECTURE

From infinite series to complex networks.

steps.narration: Have you ever wondered what the actual source code of our physical reality looks like?
It is not written in English or code, but in the rigorous language of advanced mathematics. Today, we are tearing down the blueprint.

scenes.html:
1.1 BASIC DEFINITION

SEQUENCE

A function whose domain is the set of natural numbers.

a1, a2, a3, ... an

steps.narration: Let us start at the very foundation of mathematical analysis: Sequences and Series.
A sequence is essentially a structured list. Strictly speaking, it is a function where the domain is exclusively the set of natural numbers.

scenes.html:
BOUNDED VS UNBOUNDED

BOUNDED SEQUENCE

Exists real numbers m, M such that:

m ≤ an ≤ M

UNBOUNDED SEQUENCE

A sequence is unbounded if it is not bounded by finite limits.

steps.narration: Now, not all sequences behave the same way. We categorize them by how they grow or shrink.
A sequence is bounded if it is trapped between two finite real numbers, small m and capital M. If it escapes these constraints, it is unbounded.

scenes.html:
SEQUENCE BEHAVIOR

CONVERGENT SEQUENCE

lim (n→∞) an = L (Finite)
scenes.script: (ctx) => { const getConvergent = () => Array.from({length: 30}, (_, i) => 5 + 10 * Math.exp(-0.2 * i) * Math.cos(i)); const getDivergent = () => Array.from({length: 30}, (_, i) => Math.pow(1.15, i)); const chart = ctx.chart('#seq-chart', { type: 'line', data: { labels: Array.from({length:30}, (_,i)=>i+1), datasets: [{ label: 'a_n', data: getConvergent(), borderColor: '#3b82f6', backgroundColor: '#3b82f6', pointRadius: 4, tension: 0.3 }] }, options: { responsive: true, maintainAspectRatio: false, scales: { y: { grid: { color: '#333' } }, x: { grid: { color: '#333' } } }, plugins: { legend: { display: false } } } }); return { showDivergent: () => { chart.data.datasets[0].data = getDivergent(); chart.data.datasets[0].borderColor = '#ef4444'; chart.data.datasets[0].backgroundColor = '#ef4444'; chart.update(); ctx.root.querySelector('#chart-title').innerText = 'DIVERGENT SEQUENCE'; ctx.root.querySelector('#chart-title').classList.replace('text-white', 'text-rose-500'); ctx.root.querySelector('#limit-eq').innerText = 'Limit does not exist or is infinite'; }, remove: () => chart.destroy() } }

steps.narration: If we map a sequence towards infinity, what happens? If it approaches a specific, finite real number L, we call it convergent.
But if that limit does not exist, or if it shoots off into infinity, it is a divergent sequence. It completely escapes calculation.

scenes.html:
OSCILLATORY SEQUENCE

Bounded, but limit does not exist.

an = (-1)n
+1 -1
scenes.script: (ctx) => { let toggle = true; let interval; return { startOscillate: () => { interval = setInterval(() => { const pos = ctx.root.querySelector('#val-pos'); const neg = ctx.root.querySelector('#val-neg'); if(toggle) { pos.classList.replace('opacity-100', 'opacity-20'); neg.classList.replace('opacity-20', 'opacity-100'); } else { pos.classList.replace('opacity-20', 'opacity-100'); neg.classList.replace('opacity-100', 'opacity-20'); } toggle = !toggle; }, 800); }, remove: () => clearInterval(interval) } }

steps.narration: And then there is the peculiar case of oscillatory sequences.
Take negative one to the power of n. It is bounded between negative one and positive one, but because it constantly flips, it never actually settles on a limit.

scenes.html:
1.2 SERIES EXPANSION

MACLAURIN SERIES

log(1 + x) = x - x2/2 + x3/3 - ...
REGION OF EXPANSION
-1 < x ≤ 1

steps.narration: Moving from sequences to series, we can represent complex functions as infinite sums of polynomials.

For example, the Maclaurin Series for log of one plus x expands infinitely. However, this expansion is only valid mathematically within a specific region: x strictly greater than negative one, and less than or equal to one.

scenes.html:
1.4 CONVERGENCE TESTS

D'Alembert's Ratio Test

lim | an+1 / an | = L

Cauchy's Root Test

lim n√|an| = L

Decision Matrix

L < 1 : Convergent L > 1 : Divergent L = 1 : Inconclusive

steps.narration: To determine if an infinite series converges, mathematicians developed rigorous tests.

D'Alembert's Ratio Test looks at the limit of the ratio of consecutive terms. Cauchy's Root Test takes the nth root. In both cases, if the resulting limit L is less than one, the series converges safely.

scenes.html:
FOURIER SERIES

Expansion Conditions (Dirichlet's)

  • It is single-valued
  • It is finite
  • Finite number of discontinuities
  • Finite maxima and minima
scenes.script: (ctx) => { const sketch = p => { let angle = 0; p.setup = () => { const c = ctx.root.querySelector('#p5-wave'); p.createCanvas(c.clientWidth, c.clientHeight); }; p.draw = () => { p.clear(); p.translate(p.width/2 + 200, p.height/2); p.stroke(99, 102, 241, 150); p.noFill(); p.beginShape(); for(let i=-500; i<500; i+=10) { let y = 100 * p.sin(i * 0.02 + angle) + 50 * p.sin(i * 0.05 + angle*1.5); p.vertex(i, y); } p.endShape(); angle += 0.05; }; p.windowResized = () => { const c = ctx.root.querySelector('#p5-wave'); p.resizeCanvas(c.clientWidth, c.clientHeight); }; }; const myP5 = ctx.p5(sketch, '#p5-wave'); return { revealList: () => { [1,2,3,4].forEach((i, idx) => { setTimeout(() => { ctx.root.querySelector(`#f${i}`).classList.remove('opacity-0', 'translate-x-4'); }, idx * 400); }); }, remove: () => myP5.remove() } }

steps.narration: What if we want to represent complex periodic waveforms, like a sound wave? We use a Fourier Series.
But not every function can be expanded this way. It must be single-valued, finite, and have a finite number of discontinuities and extrema.

scenes.html:
MODULE 02

MULTIVARIABLE
CALCULUS

Expanding mathematics beyond a single dimension into planar and spatial regions.

steps.narration: Until now, we have assumed functions operate on a single variable. But the real world is three-dimensional.
Welcome to Multivariable Calculus, where we track continuous change across multiple dimensions simultaneously.

scenes.html:
Simultaneous Limit

lim (x,y)→(a,b) f(x,y)

Exists only if the limit value is the same for all paths approaching the point.

Repeated Limit

lim x→a (lim y→b f(x,y))

Evaluated sequentially. A repeated limit may exist even when a simultaneous limit does not.

steps.narration: In two dimensions, calculating a limit becomes a delicate operation.
A simultaneous, or double limit, only exists if the value approaches the same number regardless of which geometric path you take to get there. Repeated limits evaluate one axis at a time.

scenes.html:
2.2 VECTOR FIELDS

IRROTATIONAL

∇ × A = 0

Curl is zero. Meaning: No circulation.

SOLENOIDAL

∇ · A = 0

Divergence is zero. Meaning: No source or sink.

steps.narration: When dealing with vector fields, like fluid flow or electromagnetism, two fundamental properties emerge.

If a field is irrotational, its curl is zero, meaning there is no circulation or whirlpool effect. If it is solenoidal, its divergence is zero, meaning nothing is being created or destroyed; there are no sources or sinks.

scenes.html:
STATIONARY &
EXTREME POINTS

Necessary Condition: fx = 0, fy = 0

Calculate D = fxxfyy - (fxy)2
D > 0, fxx > 0 : MINIMUM
D > 0, fxx < 0 : MAXIMUM
D < 0 : SADDLE POINT

steps.narration: To find the peaks and valleys of a three-dimensional surface, we look for stationary points where the first partial derivatives are exactly zero.
Using the second derivative test, or the discriminant D, we can classify these points as local minimums, maximums, or a saddle point—where it curves up in one direction and down in the other.

scenes.html:
INTEGRAL THEOREMS

GREEN'S
THEOREM

Relates a line integral around a boundary to a double integral over the enclosed plane region.

GAUSS'S
DIVERGENCE

Flux through a closed surface equals the volume integral of divergence inside it.

STOKES'
THEOREM

Surface integral of curl over a surface equals the line integral around its boundary.

steps.narration: Multivariable calculus culminates in three monumental theorems that bridge dimensions.

Green's Theorem connects 1D boundaries to 2D areas. Gauss's Theorem links 2D surfaces to 3D volumes. And Stokes' Theorem translates the curl across a surface to the path around its edge. It is beautiful symmetry.

scenes.html:
MODULE 03

DIFFERENTIAL
EQUATIONS

The mathematical language of continuous change and dynamic systems.

steps.narration: But knowing how to measure an area isn't enough to describe a universe in motion.
For that, we need Differential Equations—the mathematical language used to model everything from planetary orbits to population growth.

scenes.html:
3.1 GENERAL FORM

CLAIRAUT'S EQUATION

y = px + f(p)
where p = dy / dx

General Solution

Contains arbitrary constant.

Singular Solution

Obtained by eliminating the constant from the general solution.

steps.narration: A fascinating class of these is Clairaut's Equation, structured as y equals px plus a function of p.
Here, p represents the derivative, dy by dx. Solving it yields a general solution with a constant, and a unique singular solution derived by eliminating that very constant.

scenes.html:
THE INTEGRATING FACTOR

NON-EXACT
EQUATION
➔
EXACT
EQUATION

By multiplying both sides, it converts an unsolvable equation into a solvable one.

steps.narration: But what happens when a differential equation is stubborn? When it is considered non-exact?
We use a mathematical master key: the Integrating Factor. By multiplying it across the entire equation, it forces a non-exact equation into an exact, solvable format.

scenes.html:
MODULE 04

GRAPH THEORY
& TREES
scenes.script: (ctx) => { const sketch = p => { let nodes = []; p.setup = () => { const c = ctx.root.querySelector('#p5-nodes'); p.createCanvas(c.clientWidth, c.clientHeight); for(let i=0; i<40; i++) { nodes.push({x: p.random(p.width), y: p.random(p.height), vx: p.random(-1,1), vy: p.random(-1,1)}); } }; p.draw = () => { p.clear(); p.stroke(249, 115, 22, 100); p.strokeWeight(1); nodes.forEach(n => { n.x += n.vx; n.y += n.vy; if(n.x<0||n.x>p.width) n.vx*=-1; if(n.y<0||n.y>p.height) n.vy*=-1; nodes.forEach(other => { let d = p.dist(n.x, n.y, other.x, other.y); if(d < 150) p.line(n.x, n.y, other.x, other.y); }); }); p.fill(249, 115, 22); p.noStroke(); nodes.forEach(n => p.circle(n.x, n.y, 6)); }; p.windowResized = () => p.resizeCanvas(ctx.root.clientWidth, ctx.root.clientHeight); }; const myP5 = ctx.p5(sketch, '#p5-nodes'); return { remove: () => myP5.remove() } }

steps.narration: Finally, we transition from the continuous to the discrete.
Graph Theory. The mathematics of relationships, networks, and routing. From the internet to artificial neural networks, this is how everything connects.

scenes.html:
4.1 BASIC GRAPH DEFINITIONS

WALK

A sequence of vertices. Repetition is allowed.

PATH

A walk with NO repeated vertices.

CIRCUIT

A closed walk.

CYCLE

A closed path.

steps.narration: To navigate a graph, we must define the rules of movement.
A 'Walk' lets you revisit vertices. A 'Path' forbids repetition. A 'Circuit' is a walk that returns to its start, and a 'Cycle' is a path that loops back.

scenes.html:
SPECIAL GRAPHS

EULERIAN

A graph containing a trail that uses every edge exactly once.

HAMILTONIAN

A graph containing a cycle that visits every vertex exactly once.

steps.narration: Some networks possess perfect symmetry.

An Eulerian graph allows you to traverse every single edge exactly once without lifting your pen. A Hamiltonian graph demands you visit every vertex exactly once. Similar concepts, fundamentally different routing.

scenes.html:
4.4 THEOREM

HANDSHAKING LEMMA

∑ deg(v) = 2E

The sum of degrees of all vertices is exactly twice the number of edges.

Therefore: The number of odd degree vertices is always even.

steps.narration: One of the most elegant proofs in Graph theory is the Handshaking Lemma.
It states that the sum of the degrees of all vertices equals twice the number of edges. Because every edge connects two vertices, the number of vertices with an odd degree must always be even.

scenes.html:
TREES

A connected graph with no cycles.

Edges in a Tree

A tree with n vertices has exactly n - 1 edges.

steps.narration: A special subset of graphs forms the backbone of computer science data structures: Trees.
A tree is simply a connected graph with absolutely no cycles. By definition, if a tree has n vertices, it must have exactly n minus one edges to remain perfectly connected without looping.

scenes.html:
4.6 ALGORITHMS

PATH & SPANNING

Kruskal's Algorithm

Sorts edges, adds the smallest edge without forming a cycle.

Prim's Algorithm

Starts from a vertex, continuously adds the smallest connecting edge.

Dijkstra's Algorithm

Uses a greedy approach to find the absolute shortest path from a source node.

steps.narration: To process these networks, we use specific traversal algorithms.

Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms are used to find Minimal Spanning Trees. Dijkstra's algorithm uses a greedy approach to map the shortest path from a source vertex. These run behind your GPS routing every day.

scenes.html:
4.7 MATRIX REPRESENTATION

ADJACENCY MATRIX

Shows vertex-to-vertex connection. Usually symmetric for undirected graphs.

0
1
0
1
1
0
1
0

INCIDENCE MATRIX

Shows relation directly between vertices and edges.

1
0
1
0
0
1
1
0

steps.narration: But algorithms don't read shapes. Computers process graphs as matrices.
An Adjacency Matrix maps vertices to other vertices, plotting ones where edges connect. An Incidence Matrix maps vertices directly to edges. This converts geometry back into pure linear algebra.

scenes.html:
THE SYNTHESIS COMPLETE

Continuous limits. Discrete structures. Absolute logic.

steps.narration: From the infinite expansions of Maclaurin series to the discrete edges of a spanning tree.
Mathematics proves that whether the system is continuous or discrete, everything is bound by absolute logic. The blueprint is perfect.
